1: Carnegie Mellon University was founded in 1900 as the Carnegie Technical Schools by Andrew Carnegie.
2: The original purpose of the Carnegie Technical Schools was to provide working-class men and women in Pittsburgh with practical skills, trades, and crafts through two- and three-year certificates in arts and engineering disciplines. This initiative aimed to enhance their careers, lives, and communities. The schools also included a college for women, Margaret Morrison Carnegie College.
4: In 1967, Carnegie Tech merged with the Mellon Institute of Industrial Research to form Carnegie Mellon University.
5: The founders of the Mellon Institute of Industrial Research were Andrew Mellon and Richard B.
6: The motto of Carnegie Mellon University is "My heart is in the work.
7: Carnegie Mellon University is located in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, United States, approximately 5 miles (8 km) from downtown Pittsburgh.
8: Carnegie Mellon University comprises seven colleges and schools: the College of Engineering, the College of Fine Arts, the Dietrich College of Humanities & Social Sciences, the Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy, the Mellon College of Science, the School of Computer Science, and the Tepper School of Business.

10: The School of Computer Science (SCS) at Carnegie Mellon University in Pittsburgh is renowned for its computer science program. It has been consistently ranked among the top programs, with the graduate program tied for No. 1 with MIT, Stanford, and UC Berkeley according to the 2024 U.S.
11: The name of Carnegie Mellon's business school is the Tepper School of Business.
12: Andy Warhol is the notable artist and alumnus of Carnegie Mellon University associated with the Pop Art movement. He graduated with a BFA in 1949.

15: The official colors of Carnegie Mellon University are cardinal and gray
16: The annual event where Carnegie Mellon students build and race buggies is called Sweepstakes, which is a highlight of Spring Carnival.
17: The Fence at Carnegie Mellon University is a concrete and steel structure located in the middle of the Cut, dating back to 1923. It originally served as a meeting spot for students and became famous for its frequent painting, which includes witty messages, event advertisements, and accomplishment announcements. The original wooden fence held the Guinness World Record for being the most painted object until it collapsed in 1993 due to excessive paint buildup.

21: Randy Pausch's "Last Lecture," delivered on September 18, 2007, at Carnegie Mellon University, became known worldwide for its inspirational message on achieving childhood dreams and leaving a positive legacy despite his terminal illness diagnosis. The lecture was titled "Really Achieving Your Childhood Dreams" and was later adapted into a book that became a bestseller. Pausch discussed themes of embracing obstacles, appreciating mentors, and focusing on adding value to others' lives.
22: Andy Bechtolsheim co-founded Sun Microsystems. He earned his M.S. from Carnegie Mellon University in 1976.
23: Carnegie Mellon's annual spring carnival is called Spring Carnival
24: The distinctive cylindrical building on Carnegie Mellon University's campus is the Hamerschlag Hall, known for its rotunda which is one of the most iconic images on campus.
25: The Kiltie Band is the concert and marching band of Carnegie Mellon University, known for wearing kilts in honor of Andrew Carnegie's Scottish-American heritage. Founded in 1908, it supports Carnegie Mellon's athletic events, performing at all home football games and acting as a pep band for basketball teams. The band also hosts holiday and spring concerts.
26: The Robotics Institute (RI) at Carnegie Mellon University focuses on robotics research. Established in 1979 by Raj Reddy, it is a division of the School of Computer Science and is recognized as one of the world's leading robotics research facilities. The institute conducts research in various fields including space robotics, medical robotics, industrial systems, computer vision, and artificial intelligence.
27: The interdisciplinary program combining technology and the arts at Carnegie Mellon University is called IDeATe, which stands for Integrative Design, Arts, and Technology network. This program offers minors and courses that integrate various disciplines such as Game Design, Animation & Special Effects, Media Design, Sonic Arts, and others. Students can supplement their major with these interdisciplinary minors to build expertise in emerging fields.
28: Scott Fahlman, a Carnegie Mellon alumnus, is known for creating the emoticon ':-)'. He introduced it in 1982 on an online bulletin board at Carnegie Mellon to help distinguish jokes from serious posts.
29: Scotty Saturday is a daylong celebration at Carnegie Mellon University featuring CMU-themed activities, student talent showcases, free snacks, and giveaways. It is a day dedicated to fostering the Carnegie Mellon spirit among students and the community.
30: The main administrative hub of Carnegie Mellon University is the David and Susan Coulter Welcome Center. This building serves as the primary hub for campus visits and tours.
31: The Mobot competition at Carnegie Mellon is an annual event where robots compete to autonomously navigate through a slalom course marked on the sidewalk outside Wean Hall. It debuted in 1994 and involves robots passing through gates in order to reach the finish line. The robots use a white line on the pavement to find the gates, though they do not strictly follow it, especially towards the end where lines split and merge randomly.

39: The Plaid Parliament of Pwning (PPP) is a winning hacking team at Carnegie Mellon University that competes in Capture the Flag (CTF) cybersecurity competitions. Composed of students from various departments at CMU, the team is coached by alumni and faculty. PPP holds the distinction of being the only team to win four consecutive MITRE Embedded Capture-the-flag (eCTF) competitions.
40: The Carnegie Mellon University School of Drama is renowned for its contributions to the arts. It offers undergraduate BFA degrees in Drama with various concentrations and MFA degrees in specialized theater areas. The school is housed in the Purnell Center for the Arts, which provides state-of-the-art facilities for performance and production.
41: The Mobot competition at CMU is an annual event where autonomous robots compete to navigate a course by passing through gates in sequence and reaching the finish line. It debuted in 1994 and takes place during Spring Carnival. Robots typically follow a white line on the pavement connecting the gates but must also determine the correct path when lines split and merge towards the end of the course.
42: Andy Bechtolsheim co-founded Sun Microsystems. He is a CMU alumnus who earned his M.S. from the university.
43: Sweepstakes is the CMU tradition involving a race with uniquely designed vehicles. During Spring Carnival, students build and race aerodynamic "buggies" at speeds up to 35 mph around Schenley Park.
44: The Mall is a significant grassy area on Carnegie Mellon University's campus that runs perpendicular to The Cut, forming a cross-axis layout. This design creates a central spine for the campus, enhancing its navigability and providing a gathering space for students and events. Its location and layout contribute to the aesthetic and functional organization of the campus, reflecting the intentional planning of the university's physical environment.
45: Vint Cerf, known as one of the "Fathers of the Internet," is a CMU alumnus who is renowned for his work in computer networking and the development of the ARPANET.
46: Spring Carnival is the oldest tradition at Carnegie Mellon University, held annually over three days typically in April. It features student-built booths, racing robots, live entertainment, and various food options. A key highlight is Sweepstakes, where students build and race aerodynamic "buggies" at speeds up to 35 mph.

48: The Andrew File System (AFS), developed at Carnegie Mellon University, is a distributed file system designed to provide secure access to files over a network. It was part of the larger Andrew Project, which aimed to create a comprehensive computing environment for the university. AFS allowed users to store and access files across multiple servers, providing features like transparent caching, security, and administrative ease.

51: The NavLab project at CMU was significant because it led to the development of autonomous vehicles, including Navlab 5, which drove autonomously from Pittsburgh to San Diego in 1995, marking the longest continuous test of an autonomous vehicle in a real-world setting. This project advanced the field of autonomous driving technology and demonstrated the capability of self-navigating automobiles.
52: Carnegie Mellon University contributed to the development of speech recognition technology by creating SPHINX, the first real-time speech recognition system using AI techniques, developed by Kai-Fu Lee, Raj Reddy, and Roberto Bisiani. Additionally, CMU's Language Technologies Institute (LTI) focuses on speech recognition among other language technologies, continuing to advance the field through interdisciplinary research.

54: The DARPA Grand Challenge was significant for CMU because it showcased the university's expertise in autonomous vehicle technology. In 2007, CMU's Tartan Racing team, led by roboticist Red Whittaker, won the DARPA Urban Challenge with their self-driving SUV "Boss." This victory demonstrated CMU's leadership in developing advanced autonomous systems capable of navigating complex urban environments while adhering to traffic laws. The success of Boss highlighted CMU's contributions to the field of robotics and solidified its reputation as a pioneer in artificial intelligence and autonomous vehicle research.
55: Carnegie Mellon University played a pivotal role in the development of self-driving taxis through its groundbreaking research in autonomous vehicles. Additionally, the university's Navlab 5 project, led by Dean Pomerleau and Todd Jochem, successfully drove autonomously from Pittsburgh to San Diego, showcasing long-distance autonomous navigation. 56: The Iris rover, developed at Carnegie Mellon University, was designed for lunar exploration and played a role in planetary exploration.
57: Libratus is an artificial intelligence developed by researchers at Carnegie Mellon University that achieved superhuman performance in the complex game of heads-up no-limit poker. It was created by a team led by Tuomas Sandholm, a professor at CMU. Libratus was notable for defeating four top professional poker players in a 20-day competition in 2017.
58: Pittsburgh Restaurant Week is an annual event that celebrates dining in Pittsburgh, featuring winter and summer editions in January and August respectively. During this event, participating restaurants create limited-time menus offering special discounts and dining experiences. The winter edition focuses on new dishes for the new year, while the summer edition highlights local produce and lighter fare.
59: Bloomfield is known for its Italian heritage and hosts Little Italy Days. The festival takes place over four days in August.
60: The Pittsburgh Taco Festival is an annual food festival held in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, celebrating tacos and other Mexican-inspired foods. The festival includes live music, family-friendly activities, and supports local non-profits aiding Pittsburgh’s Latin American community.
61: The Great American Banana Split Celebration in Latrobe celebrates the city's love for the banana split. This festival includes live music, goat yoga, kids activities, a Yellow Tie Gala, pie-eating contests, and a 5k banana run. It has been a staple in Westmoreland County since 2013.

108: Pittsburgh was named in 1758 by Scottish General John Forbes in honor of British statesman William Pitt, 1st Earl of Chatham. Forbes, being a Scotsman, likely pronounced the name similar to "PITS-bər-ə," akin to how Edinburgh is pronounced. The settlement between the rivers was initially referred to as "Pittsborough." The name was officially adopted when the town was incorporated as a borough in 1794.
109: Pittsburgh is located in southwestern Pennsylvania, at the confluence of the Allegheny and Monongahela rivers, where they form the Ohio River. The city is situated within the boundaries of Appalachia and is considered part of both Northeastern and Midwestern regions due to its geographical position. Pittsburgh lies within the Pittsburgh Combined Statistical Area, which includes parts of Ohio and West Virginia.
110: Pittsburgh is known as "the Steel City" due to its historical dominance in the U.S.
111: The Native American tribes that originally inhabited the Pittsburgh region included the Paleo-Indians, followed by the Adena and Hopewell cultures. By 1700, the Haudenosaunee (Five Nations), Lenape, and Shawnee were prominent. Other tribes like the Delaware also migrated to the area.
112: A significant event that took place at Fort Duquesne was its capture by the British in November 25, 1758, following its destruction and abandonment by the French.
114: The year 1758 is significant in Pittsburgh's history because it marked the successful expulsion of the French from Fort Duquesne by British General John Forbes and his army. This event ended French control over the upper Ohio Valley and secured the area for British dominance. Following the French departure, Forbes renamed the site Pittsburgh and ordered the construction of Fort Pitt to ensure continued British control. This victory paved the way for increased European settlement in the region, transforming it into a strategic gateway to the west.
115: The Allegheny and Monongahela rivers converge to form the Ohio River in Pittsburgh. This confluence occurs at Point State Park in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.
116: The Whiskey Rebellion was a violent tax protest in the United States from 1791 to 1794, primarily against a federal excise tax on whiskey. This tax, the first on a domestic product, was imposed to pay off the national debt from the American Revolutionary War. Western Pennsylvania farmers, who relied heavily on whiskey production, resisted the tax through violence and intimidation.
117: Pittsburgh's geography contributed to its industrial growth through its strategic location at the confluence of the Allegheny and Monongahela rivers, forming the Ohio River. This location facilitated river transportation, enabling easy access to markets downstream and beyond. Additionally, the region's abundant natural resources, particularly coal and iron ore, supported the development of the iron and steel industries. The availability of railroads and rivers for transporting raw materials and finished goods further enhanced the city's industrial capabilities. Pittsburgh's geography thus created a favorable environment for industrial expansion and economic prosperity.
118: During the American Civil War, Pittsburgh played a significant role by boosting its economy through increased demand for iron and armaments by the Union. The city's industrial capabilities contributed to the war effort, supporting the Union's military needs.
120: The Fort Pitt Blockhouse is Pittsburgh's oldest architectural landmark, built in 1764 as part of Fort Pitt during the French and Indian War. It is the sole remaining structure of the original fort and stands as a testament to western Pennsylvania's historical significance during the colonial period. Gifted to the Fort Pitt Society of the Daughters of the American Revolution in 1894, the Blockhouse has been preserved and remains open to visitors free of charge.
121: The H.J. Heinz Company was founded in Pittsburgh in 1869
123: After World War II, Pittsburgh's economy shifted away from heavy industry towards diversification. The steel industry, which had been dominant, began to decline due to foreign competition and reduced demand. The city focused on developing light industries, high-tech sectors like computer software and robotics, and services. Industrial research labs were established, contributing to the city's transformation. Despite challenges, Pittsburgh avoided the fate of many Rust Belt cities by successfully adapting its economic base.
124: The 'Renaissance' period in Pittsburgh's history refers to the city's clean air and civic revitalization project that began following World War II, specifically starting in 1946 and lasting until 1973, known as Renaissance I. This period aimed to transform Pittsburgh from the "Smoky City" by implementing smoke control measures and urban renewal projects. Key initiatives included demolishing old buildings and constructing new developments like Gateway Center, the U.S.
125: The University of Pittsburgh was founded in 1787
126: In 1964, the Pittsburgh Pirates won the World Series, marking their fifth championship.
127: Pittsburgh has 446 bridges, more than any other city in the world, including Venice, which is known as the "City of Bridges." Approximately 40 of these bridges cross the three rivers near the city center. The total number of bridges in Allegheny County exceeds 2,200.
128: Point State Park is a 36-acre park located at the tip of Pittsburgh's Golden Triangle, where the Allegheny and Monongahela rivers meet to form the Ohio River. The park features expansive lawns, riverfront trails, and the famous fountain that sprays water up to 150 feet into the air. It opened in 1974 and includes historical sites like the Fort Pitt Blockhouse, believed to be the oldest building in Pittsburgh.
129: Homewood is known for its cultural diversity. It celebrates Black culture and heritage through various events and is highlighted for its community support and historical contributions to Black cultural excellence.
130: The Cathedral of Learning is a 42-story, 535-foot-tall Late Gothic Revival skyscraper serving as the centerpiece of the University of Pittsburgh's main campus in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. It is the tallest educational building in the Western Hemisphere and houses the Dietrich School of Arts and Sciences, the School of Social Work, and the Frederick Honors College. The building includes over 2,000 rooms, 31 Nationality Rooms, and a large gothic Commons Room used for study and events.
131: Alcoa is the Pittsburgh-based company known for its aluminum products
132: The Homestead Strike was a significant labor confrontation in 1892 at Carnegie Steel's Homestead plant, marking one of the most serious conflicts in U.S. labor history. It arose from a dispute between the Amalgamated Association of Iron and Steel Workers and Carnegie Steel Company over wages and working conditions. The strike lasted 143 days and involved violent clashes, including the Battle of Homestead. Despite the immediate defeat of the workers, the event contributed to the growth of modern labor practices and laws protecting workers.

141: Andrew Carnegie was a Scottish-American industrialist and philanthropist who played a pivotal role in developing the U.S. steel industry, particularly in Pittsburgh. He founded the Carnegie Steel Company, which contributed significantly to Pittsburgh's growth as a major industrial center. Carnegie's influence extended beyond business; he donated substantial sums to establish cultural institutions like the Carnegie Museums of Pittsburgh and educational facilities such as Carnegie Mellon University. His philanthropic efforts reshaped Pittsburgh's landscape, enhancing its cultural and educational resources.
142: Lawrenceville is known for its vibrant arts scene, featuring many artists and designers.
143: Unique to Pittsburgh's Inclines is their role as both historical transportation systems and modern tourist attractions, offering iconic views of the city's skyline. The Duquesne Incline features red cars and is visible from The Point, while the Monongahela Incline, known as "The Mon Incline," has yellow cars and can be seen from the South Shore. These inclines, originally opened in the 1870s, are the last remaining of four that once served Mount Washington, providing a blend of practicality and scenic beauty.
144: The Nationality Rooms program at the University of Pittsburgh is a series of classrooms within the Cathedral of Learning, each designed by different cultural groups that significantly contributed to Pittsburgh's development. These rooms celebrate various cultures and depict eras before or around 1787. The program started in 1926 when Chancellor John Gabbert Bowman invited ethnic communities to design and fund their respective rooms, with the university providing only the space and subsequent maintenance.
145: The longest bridge in Pittsburgh is the Fort Pitt Bridge, also known as the 3rd Street Bridge, with a length of approximately 3,080 feet. However, since specific lengths for all bridges aren't provided in the context, the Roberto Clemente Bridge, with a span of 440 feet for its main sections, is noted as one of the significant bridges in the city. For the longest single bridge strictly within the given context, the Salisbury Viaduct with a length of 1,908 feet is the longest mentioned.
146: The Smithfield Street Bridge is significant as it is the oldest river crossing in Pittsburgh, opening in 1883, and it holds the distinction of being the world's first lenticular truss bridge. Designated as both a Historic Civil Engineering Landmark and a National Historic Landmark, it played a crucial role in establishing Pittsburgh as the "City of Bridges." Additionally, it offers great views of the city from various angles.
147: Primanti Brothers sandwiches are famously associated with Pittsburgh. These sandwiches feature meat, cheese, hand-cut French fries, tomatoes, and coleslaw between two slices of Italian bread. Another notable local specialty is pierogies.
148: The Allegheny County Courthouse serves as the primary location for the judicial proceedings of Allegheny County, housing the Court of Common Pleas and Magisterial District Courts. 151: Carnegie Mellon University is known for robotics research. 152: The Golden Triangle is the colloquial name for Downtown Pittsburgh, also known as the Central Business District. It is located at the confluence of the Allegheny and Monongahela Rivers, where they meet to form the Ohio River. Bounded by these rivers and Interstate 579 (Crosstown Boulevard) to the east, this triangular area is the urban downtown center of Pittsburgh.
